analogy: Think of "Structural Change" through the chapter's own opening images: Social Change and Development in India 2 Understanding the present usually involves some grasp of its past. This holds true probably as much for an individual or social group as for an entire country such as India. While knowing about its past in ancient and... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 1.1 Understanding Colonialism, 1.2 Urbanisation and indUstrialisation, Structural Change, Structural. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Structural Change is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that social Change and Development in India 2 Understanding the present usually involves some grasp of its past.

The text explains that this holds true probably as much for an individual or social group as for an entire country such as India.

The text explains that while knowing about its past in ancient and medieval times is very important, its colonial experience is particularly significant for comprehending modern India.

The text explains that this is not just because many modern ideas and institutions reached India through colonialism.

The text explains that it is also because such an exposure to modern ideas was contradictory or paradoxical.

The text explains that for example, Indians in the colonial period read about western liberalism and freedom.

The text explains that yet they lived under a western, colonial rule that denied Indians liberty and freedom.

The text explains that it is contradictions of this kind that shaped many of the structural and cultural changes that Chapters 1 and 2 look at.

The text explains that as the next few chapters shall show, our social reform and nationalist movements, our laws, our political life and our Constitution, our industry and agriculture, our cities and our villages have been shaped by our paradoxical experience with colonialism.

The text explains that this has had lasting implications for our specific experience with modernity.

The text explains that the following are just some of the many instances we face in our daily life.

The text explains that we have a parliamentary and a legal system, a police and educational system built very much on the British model.

The text explains that we drive on the left side of the road like the British.

The text explains that we have ‘bread-omlette’ and ‘cutlets’ as menu offered in many roadside eateries and canteens.

The text explains that a very popular manufacturer of biscuits, is actually named after Britain.

The text explains that we often admire the west and as often resent it.

The text explains that in this chapter we focus on structural changes that colonialism brought in.

The text explains that we, therefore, need to shift from this broad impressionistic view to a clearer understanding of colonialism as a structure and system.

The text explains that in this chapter we look at only two of these structural changes namely industrialisation and urbanisation.

The text explains that all these structural changes were accompanied by cultural changes which, we look at in the next chapter.

The text explains that virtually English Housewives and college students who know English take up plum assignments as online scorers in BPOs, writes K.

The text explains that online education, the new wave in the BPO segment, is bringing cheer to those who want to earn a fast buck.

The text explains that history is full of examples of the annexation of foreign territory and the domination of weaker by stronger powers.

The text explains that apart from outright pillage, the pre-capitalist conquerors benefited from their domination by exacting a continuous flow of tribute.

The text explains that (Alavi and Shanin, 1982) In contrast British colonialism which was based on a capitalist system directly interfered to ensure greatest profit and benefit to British capitalism.

The text explains that a newly emerging middle class particularly from the British Presidency regions of Bengal and Madras moved as government employees and professionals like doctors and lawyers moved to different parts of the country.

The text explains that this magnitude and depth of the structural changes that colonialism unleashed can be better grasped if we try and understand some basic features of capitalism.

The text explains that capitalism is an economic system in which the means of production are privately owned and organised to accumulate profits within a market system.

The text explains that (We have already discussed the capitalist market in the first book – Indian Society.) Capitalism in the west emerged out of a complex process of European exploration of the rest of the world, its plunder of wealth and resources, an unprecedented growth of science and technology, its harnessing to industries and agriculture.

The text explains that if capitalism became the dominant economic system, nation states became the dominant political form.

The text explains that a government has sovereign power After 1834 till 1920, ships left from the ports of India on regular basis carrying people of various religions, gender, classes and castes destined to work for a minimum of five years on one of the plantations in Mauritius.

The text explains that the principle of nationalism assumes that any set of people have a right to be free and exercise sovereign power.

The text explains that you will be reading more about this in Chapter 3.

The text explains that it must have struck you that the practice of colonialism and the principle of nationalism and democratic rights are contradictory.

The text explains that nationalism implied that the people of India or of any colonised society have an equal right to be sovereign.

The text explains that they declared that freedom or swaraj was their birthright and fought for both political and economic freedom.

The text explains that 1.2 Urbanisation and indUstrialisation The Colonial experienCe Industrialisation refers to the emergence of machine production, based on the use of inanimate power resources like steam or electricity.

The text explains that by contrast, a prime feature of industrial societies today is that a large majority of the employed population work in factories, offices or shops rather than agriculture.

The text explains that for instance in Britain, the first society to undergo industrialisation, was also the earliest to move from being rural to a predominantly urban country.

The text explains that village crafts in the interior, and particularly, in regions other than eastern India where British penetration was earliest and deepest, probably survived much longer, coming to be seriously affected only with the spread of railways.

The text explains that it also shows how industrialisation is not just about new machine based production but also a story of the growth of new social groups in society and new social relationships.

The text explains that in other words it is about changes in the Indian social structure.

The text explains that social Change and Development in India 8 Cities had a key role in the economic system of empires.

The text explains that c o l o n i a l c i t i e s were the prime link between the economic centre or core in Britain and periphery or margins in colonised India.

The text explains that the fort and the open area (called Maidan) formed the core of the city that emerged rather rapidly.  Find out more about the beginnings of the three cities.  Find out also more about the story of the names they were called by leading to the very recent changes from Bombay to Mumbai, Madras to Chennai, Calcutta to Kolkata, Bangalore to Bengaluru.  Find out about the growth of other colonial urban centres. aCTiviTy 1.1 The substitutes offered by the East India Company and subsequently by the British government were land ownership and facilities for education in English.

The text explains that official reports show how the colonial government often used unfair means to hire and forcibly keep labourers.

The text explains that instead, they had recourse to fraud and coercion; and they persuaded the government to aid and abet them in this unholy task by passing penal laws. …The recruitment of labourers for tea gardens of Assam was carried on for years mostly by contractors under the provisions of the Transport of Native Labourers Act (No.

The text explains that chapter 5 will deal with Indian industrialisation and its shift from the early years of independence to developments after 1990 with liberalisation.

The text explains that the Swadeshi movement strengthened the loyalty to the national economy.

The text explains that industrialisation and urbanisation are linked processes.

The text explains that identify any town or city with which you are familiar.

The text explains that find out both the history of its growth and its contemporary status.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Social Change and Development in India, Chapter 1: Structural Change

prelims_traps: Do not treat 1.1 Understanding Colonialism and 1.2 Urbanisation and indUstrialisation as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Structural Change; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Structural Change through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 1.1 Understanding Colonialism and 1.2 Urbanisation and indUstrialisation to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
